intro.value: This reference first explains the general eigenvalue idea, then applies it to Eq. 17.
The unknowns are not just symbols inside the equation. You are solving for a perturbation shape mu and a number lambda that tells whether that shape decays, stays neutral, or grows.

content.value: An eigenvalue problem starts with something that acts on an input. In finite-dimensional algebra that something is usually a matrix A, and the input is a vector v.

This equation asks for special nonzero inputs v that keep the same direction after A acts on them. The output may be larger, smaller, reversed, or rotated in a complex sense, but it is still the same basic mode multiplied by one number lambda.

reference.value: The special input v is the eigenvector.
The scale factor lambda is the eigenvalue.
The zero vector is excluded because A times zero equals lambda times zero for every lambda, so it would not identify a meaningful mode.
In stability problems, the same idea is used with modes of motion: each mode has a lambda telling how that mode evolves.

content.value: The entire left side of Eq. 17 can be read as one linear operator acting on the perturbation shape mu.
This is the same structural idea as matrix eigenvalues, except the object acting on mu is a differential-integral operator rather than a finite matrix.

reference.value: Matrix version: A acts on v and returns the same direction v multiplied by lambda.
Eq. 17 version: L acts on the perturbation shape mu and returns the same shape multiplied by lambda.

sections.title: What the Eigenfunction Is

content.value: The eigenfunction is mu of theta and omega. It is the spatial-frequency shape of the disturbance around the incoherent density.
The primed copy mu of theta-prime and omega-prime inside the integral is the same kind of shape sampled at dummy integration variables.

sections.referenceTitle: Do Not Read as One Number

reference.value: In Eq. 17, mu is not a scalar coefficient. It is a function over phase theta and natural frequency omega.
The equation asks which function shapes can reproduce themselves after the linearized dynamics acts on them.

content.value: Lambda appears because the perturbation was separated into a fixed shape times an exponential time factor.
That means lambda is the growth rate attached to the shape mu. Positive real part means the disturbance amplifies. Negative real part means it damps out.

reference.value: The stability question is about the real part of lambda because e to a complex lambda t grows or decays according to its real part.

content.value: You are looking for admissible pairs: a nonzero perturbation shape mu and a matching lambda.
Eq. 18 is carried along because not every function shape is allowed. The perturbation must preserve normalization, so its phase average must be zero for each omega.

reference.value: If any admissible eigenvalue has positive real part, incoherence is linearly unstable.
If all admissible eigenvalues have negative real part, small perturbations decay and incoherence is linearly stable.
